Content_Sketch: Screen glowing in a dark trailer. Hand on joystick. Aerial view of a village from 10000 feet.
Illustration_Note: POV from operator's perspective: multiple glowing screens in dark GCS trailer, hand on joystick, aerial infrared view of a village on main screen

Content_Sketch: You have never set foot here. But you know every person by name. You mapped their routines. You logged their prayers.
Illustration_Note: Stickman operator (dot eyes, headset) seated in dark GCS, face illuminated by screen glow, surveillance feed showing distant village with tiny figures

Content_Sketch: You sign up for the Air Force at 18. You dream of fighter jets and open sky.
Illustration_Note: Young stickman in recruitment office, poster of fighter jet on wall behind, signing papers at desk

Content_Sketch: They assign you to Remotely Piloted Aircraft. Not a real cockpit. Not a real aircraft. That is what the other pilots say.
Illustration_Note: Split: left side shows fighter pilot climbing into jet cockpit, right side shows stickman walking toward a windowless trailer in desert

Content_Sketch: You are 22. One year of training. UPT. RPA pipeline. MQ-9 Reaper qualification. You learn to fly something you will never sit inside.
Illustration_Note: Stickman in flight suit at training console, MQ-9 Reaper drone diagram on screen, Creech AFB desert landscape visible through briefing room window

Content_Sketch: First day at Creech Air Force Base. 45 miles northwest of Las Vegas. A windowless trailer in the Nevada desert. This is your cockpit.
Illustration_Note: Wide shot of Creech AFB in desert, stickman standing before row of Ground Control Station trailers, heat waves rising from sand, mountains in background

Content_Sketch: Rotating shifts. Days. Swings. Mids. The screen never turns off. Somewhere on the other side of the planet a camera is always watching.
Illustration_Note: Interior GCS: two stickmen (operator with dot eyes + sensor operator with buzz cut) seated before wall of screens showing aerial surveillance feeds, clock showing 0300

Content_Sketch: First assignment. A man. Callsign JACKPOT. You watch him for six weeks straight. You watch him eat breakfast. Take his child to school. Pray at dawn.
Illustration_Note: Split screen effect: main monitor showing infrared view of JACKPOT (bearded figure in traditional clothing) walking with small child, operator stickman watching intently

Content_Sketch: You know his sleep schedule. You know his Friday dinner recipe. You know his youngest plays in the front yard every afternoon. He does not know you exist.
Illustration_Note: Close on operator stickman's face (dot eyes staring), green-tinted screen reflection on face, small surveillance image of JACKPOT playing with child in yard

Content_Sketch: Pattern of Life. That is the official term. You document a man's entire life to determine the right moment to end it.
Illustration_Note: Dark background, text 'PATTERN OF LIFE' in military stencil font, faint surveillance grid overlay beneath text

Content_Sketch: You drive home at 6 PM. Past schools. Grocery stores. Parks. Your wife asks how was your day. Fine. Your son asks what you do. Dad works with computers.
Illustration_Note: Split: left shows suburban Las Vegas street with stickman driving past school and park, right shows same stickman at dinner table with wife (shoulder-length hair) and son (short hair), mundane domestic scene

Content_Sketch: The fighter pilots call you chair force. Your war does not count. You have never been shot at. You have never run. But every morning you sit down and hold a life in your hands.
Illustration_Note: Group of fighter pilot stickmen (faceless) laughing in break room, operator stickman (dot eyes, headset around neck) standing apart, looking away

Content_Sketch: Psychological whiplash. The military doctor will call it that later. You do not know the name yet. You just know you talk less and less.
Illustration_Note: Operator stickman at home on couch, family in background (wife, son), stickman staring forward with dot eyes, gap between him and family growing

Content_Sketch: Week seven. JACKPOT is sitting in his yard with his family. His wife is pouring tea. The little one runs around the table. The headset crackles: Strike authorized. Cleared hot.
Illustration_Note: GCS interior: operator stickman gripping joystick with both hands, screen showing aerial view of JACKPOT with family (3 small figures) sitting around table in courtyard

Content_Sketch: You see the child sit down next to his father. You ask: Confirm - civilians in compound. The answer comes back: Acknowledged. Cleared hot. Execute.
Illustration_Note: POV through screen: zoomed surveillance feed, JACKPOT and child figure sitting close together, targeting crosshair centered, radio text overlay: CLEARED HOT

Content_Sketch: Three seconds. You press. Hellfire drops. Eight seconds of flight. The screen flashes white. Then it returns. The yard has no table. No chairs. No one sitting anymore.
Illustration_Note: Screen showing aftermath: white flash fading to dust cloud over courtyard, empty crater where table was, operator stickman frozen in chair, sensor operator (buzz cut) removing headset

Content_Sketch: Shift ends. You drive to McDonald's. Buy a Happy Meal for your daughter. She runs to hug your legs at the door. She is the same age as the child on the screen.
Illustration_Note: Split: left shows operator stickman at McDonald's counter holding Happy Meal box, right shows daughter stickman (hair bow) running to hug his legs at front door of suburban home

Content_Sketch: Days after. You still show up. Still sit in the chair. Still turn on the screen. But your hands shake. Strike two. Three. Seven. You stop counting.
Illustration_Note: GCS interior: operator stickman at same station, same screens, but posture more hunched, hands trembling on joystick, tally marks scratched on desk edge

Content_Sketch: You start drinking after shifts. Then before shifts. Your wife says: I do not recognize you anymore. You do not recognize yourself either.
Illustration_Note: Home kitchen: operator stickman alone at table with bottle, wife stickman (shoulder-length hair) standing in doorway with arms crossed, gap between them

Content_Sketch: The military doctor asks: Have you experienced combat? You do not know how to answer. You have never been shot at. But you have killed people. Many times. From a swivel chair.
Illustration_Note: Medical office: operator stickman hunched in chair facing military doctor stickman (white coat) behind desk, clinical fluorescent lighting

Content_Sketch: PTSD. The file reads non-combat related stress. Infantry veterans look at you like a fraud. You were never in danger. That is the problem.
Illustration_Note: Operator stickman sitting in VA waiting room, other veteran stickmen (some with visible injuries) nearby, operator looking down at his unmarked hands

Content_Sketch: You leave the military. IT support for an insurance company. You sit in front of a screen eight hours a day. Identical setup. But no one dies.
Illustration_Note: Split: left shows old GCS station with military screens and joystick, right shows identical seated posture at civilian office desk with keyboard and monitor, same hunched stickman in both

Content_Sketch: Your wife is gone. Your daughter calls once a week. You want to tell her what you did. But you never do. Sometimes you open Google Maps and zoom into villages you do not recognize. You do not know why.
Illustration_Note: Apartment at night: operator stickman alone at laptop, Google Maps satellite view on screen showing aerial view of a village, phone with missed call notification beside him, empty room

Content_Sketch: Another screen. Another desk. Another life you watch from a distance. But this time the screen is a laptop. The desk is in your apartment. And the life you are watching is your own.
Illustration_Note: Mirror of HOOK composition: stickman seated at desk, screens glowing, but civilian apartment instead of GCS, laptop showing ordinary email instead of surveillance, same posture as opening shot

Content_Sketch: You were safe. You were always safe. That is the worst part. If you ever wondered what it feels like to kill from ten thousand miles away - now you know.
Illustration_Note: Final frame: extreme closeup of operator stickman face (dot eyes, neutral mouth), faint green glow on face fading to natural light, still and quiet
